text.title: Guardrails Antipatterns
text.summary: Concrete antipatterns in guardrails implementation that lead to security bypasses, runaway retries, or silent failures.

antipattern.description: Catching exceptions inside validators and returning True (is_safe) on failure.
antipattern.reason: A broken validator silently approves all requests, turning a safety component into a no-op. Failed checks must propagate as violations, not as passes.

antipattern.description: Running LLM-based hallucination detection synchronously on every request regardless of stakes.
antipattern.reason: Adds 200–800ms latency and doubles LLM cost per call. Reserve for high-stakes outputs where a context document is available; use async post-processing for review.

antipattern.description: Trusting inter-agent messages implicitly and not running input guardrails on tool results.
antipattern.reason: Prompt injection can arrive through tool outputs, not just user messages. An agent that validates user input but trusts tool results is vulnerable to injection via poisoned tool responses.

antipattern.description: Logging the full violating content including PII to a shared log store.
antipattern.reason: Audit logs must preserve evidence of the violation type without leaking the PII that triggered it. Hash or truncate violating content before writing to logs.

antipattern.description: Retrying indefinitely on injection detection without a max-attempts block.
antipattern.reason: A genuinely adversarial user will send the same injection forever. Agents must cap retry attempts and escalate to human review or rate-limit the source.

antipattern.description: Using regex-only PII detection without a purpose-built library like Microsoft Presidio.
antipattern.reason: Regex-only PII detection has unacceptably high false-negative rates for real-world content. Presidio combines NER models, regex, and context rules for production-grade accuracy.
